compute-options.title: Amazon EC2 vs AWS Lambda vs Amazon ECS vs Amazon EKS vs AWS Fargate
compute-options.subtitle: Compare core compute options: virtual servers, serverless, containers, and managed orchestration

values: You manage EC2 or use Fargate
You manage EC2 nodes or use Fargate

studyAids.mnemonic: L-E-C-F: L(ambda=event,15min) + E(C2=full control) + C(ontainers=ECS/EKS) + F(argate=serverless containers)
studyAids.studyTips: Know the compute spectrum: EC2 (full control) -> ECS/EKS (containers) -> Lambda (serverless) -> Fargate (serverless containers). EC2 purchasing options are exam favorites.

examKeywords.ec2: Full control, OS access, Custom runtime, Long-running, SSH access
examKeywords.lambda: Event-driven, Pay per invocation, No servers, 15 min timeout, Cold start
examKeywords.ecs: AWS-native containers, Task definitions, Services, Fargate compatible
examKeywords.eks: Kubernetes, K8s workloads, Portability, Pods, Cluster
examKeywords.fargate: Serverless containers, No EC2 management, Per-vCPU billing

distractorClues.ec2: Wrong when question says no server management -> use Lambda/Fargate
distractorClues.lambda: Wrong for long-running processes (>15 min) -> use EC2/ECS
distractorClues.ecs: Wrong if they need Kubernetes -> use EKS. Wrong if no server mgmt -> use Fargate
distractorClues.eks: Wrong if they want AWS-native simplicity -> use ECS
distractorClues.fargate: Wrong if they need underlying host access -> use ECS on EC2

ec2-pricing.title: On-Demand vs Reserved Instances vs Spot Instances vs Savings Plans vs Dedicated Hosts
ec2-pricing.subtitle: Compare EC2 purchasing options: flexibility, cost, and commitment levels

studyAids.mnemonic: SIR-SD: S(pot=batch 90%) + I(ntelligent Reserved=72%) + R(Savings Plans=flexible 72%) + S(hared vs Dedicated) + D(edicated Hosts=compliance)
studyAids.studyTips: Savings Plans > Reserved Instances for flexibility. Spot for fault-tolerant. On-Demand for short-term. Dedicated Hosts for compliance.

examKeywords.od: No commitment, Pay per hour, Short-term, Unpredictable
examKeywords.ri: 1-3 year commitment, 72% off, Steady-state, Upfront options
examKeywords.sp: Flexible commitment, Cross instance family, 72% off
examKeywords.dh: Dedicated hardware, Compliance, Licensing, Physical server

distractorClues.od: Wrong for steady-state 24/7 -> use Reserved/Savings Plans
distractorClues.ri: Wrong for unpredictable workloads -> use On-Demand or Spot
distractorClues.spot: Wrong for workloads that cannot tolerate interruption -> use On-Demand/Reserved
distractorClues.sp: Wrong if no commitment -> use On-Demand
distractorClues.dh: Wrong if no compliance requirement -> use shared tenancy (cheaper)

container-services.title: Amazon ECS vs Amazon EKS vs AWS Fargate vs App Runner
container-services.subtitle: Compare container services: orchestration, serverless options, and simplified deployment

studyAids.mnemonic: ECFA: E(CS=AWS native) + C(K8s=EKS) + F(argate=no servers) + A(pp Runner=simplest). Every Container Finds A home.
studyAids.studyTips: ECS = AWS-native. EKS = Kubernetes. Fargate = serverless (works with both). App Runner = simplest deploy.

examKeywords.eks: Kubernetes, K8s, Control plane, Pods, EKS Anywhere
examKeywords.apprunner: Simplest container deploy, Source-to-URL, Git integration

distractorClues.ecs: Wrong if need Kubernetes -> use EKS
distractorClues.eks: Wrong if want AWS-native simplicity -> use ECS
distractorClues.fargate: Wrong if need EC2 instance access -> use ECS on EC2
distractorClues.apprunner: Wrong for complex orchestration -> use ECS or EKS

serverless-services.title: AWS Lambda vs AWS Fargate vs Aurora Serverless vs Amazon DynamoDB vs Amazon API Gateway
serverless-services.subtitle: Compare serverless services across compute, databases, and API layers

studyAids.mnemonic: LADS-F: L(ambda=compute) + A(PI Gateway=entry) + D(yamoDB=database) + S(erverless=Aurora) + F(argate=containers)
studyAids.studyTips: Classic serverless stack: API Gateway + Lambda + DynamoDB. Aurora Serverless for intermittent SQL. Fargate for serverless containers.

examKeywords.lambda: Event-driven, 15 min timeout, Cold start, GB-sec
examKeywords.fargate: Serverless containers, No EC2, ECS/EKS compatible
examKeywords.dynamodb: Key-value, Single-digit ms, On-demand or provisioned

distractorClues.lambda: Wrong for >15 min processes -> use EC2/ECS
distractorClues.fargate: Wrong if need function-based compute -> use Lambda
distractorClues.aurora_sl: Wrong for steady high-throughput -> use provisioned Aurora
distractorClues.dynamodb: Wrong for complex SQL/joins -> use Aurora/RDS
distractorClues.apigw: Wrong for direct service-to-service -> use EventBridge/SQS

auto-scaling.title: Target Tracking vs Step Scaling vs Scheduled vs Predictive Scaling
auto-scaling.subtitle: Compare EC2 Auto Scaling policies: how they respond to demand changes

values: Uses ML to predict traffic patterns
"Scale up at 9 AM down at 6 PM"

studyAids.mnemonic: TSSP: T(arget=most common) + S(tep=thresholds) + S(cheduled=known times) + P(redictive=ML). Target Steps on Schedule Predictably.
studyAids.studyTips: Target Tracking is the default answer. Step for thresholds. Scheduled for known patterns. Predictive uses ML.

examKeywords.tt: Target value, CPU utilization, Most common, Automatic

distractorClues.tt: Wrong if specific thresholds needed -> use Step Scaling
distractorClues.ss: Wrong for simple maintain-target -> use Target Tracking
distractorClues.sc: Wrong if unpredictable traffic -> use Target Tracking
distractorClues.ps: Wrong if no historical pattern -> use Target Tracking

storage-types.title: Amazon S3 vs Amazon EBS vs Amazon EFS vs EC2 Instance Store
storage-types.subtitle: Compare object, block, file, and ephemeral storage for different use cases

studyAids.mnemonic: SOFA-B: S(3=Object) + O(ne Zone-IA=cheap infrequent) + F(Sx=Windows/HPC) + A(rchive=Glacier) + B(lock=EBS). S3 unlimited, EBS AZ-locked, EFS regional.
studyAids.studyTips: S3 is the most important storage service. Know all storage tiers. EBS is AZ-locked, EFS is regional, Instance Store is ephemeral.

examKeywords.s3: Object, Bucket, 11 nines, Regional, Unlimited, API
examKeywords.ebs: Block, Boot volume, AZ-locked, Snapshot, 64TB, io2/gp3
examKeywords.efs: NFS, Linux, Regional, Auto-growing, Shared files
examKeywords.istore: Ephemeral, Temporary, Physical disk, Lost on stop, Cache

distractorClues.s3: Wrong for boot volumes or low-latency DB storage -> use EBS
distractorClues.ebs: Wrong if data needs AZ-failure survival without replication -> use EFS or S3
distractorClues.efs: Wrong for Windows file sharing -> use FSx for Windows
distractorClues.istore: Wrong if persistence is required -> use EBS or S3

s3-storage-classes.title: S3 Storage Classes: Standard vs Standard-IA vs One Zone-IA vs Glacier Instant vs Flexible vs Deep Archive vs Intelligent-Tiering
s3-storage-classes.subtitle: Compare S3 storage tiers by access frequency, cost, and retrieval time

studyAids.mnemonic: S-O-G-I: S(tandard=frequent) + O(ne Zone-IA=cheap infrequent) + G(lacier=archive: Instant/Flexible/Deep) + I(ntelligent-Tiering=auto). SOGI sounds like soggy data.
studyAids.studyTips: Standard for frequent. Standard-IA for infrequent (30+ days). One Zone-IA cheapest infrequent but single AZ. Glacier family for archives. Intelligent-Tiering for unknown patterns.

examKeywords.it: Auto-tiering, Unknown patterns, Small monitoring fee

distractorClues.std: Wrong for infrequently accessed data -> use Standard-IA or One Zone-IA
distractorClues.sia: Wrong for data accessed less than 30 days -> use Standard (min duration fee)
distractorClues.ozia: Wrong if AZ redundancy required -> use Standard-IA
distractorClues.gi: Wrong if not needed instantly -> use Glacier Flexible (cheaper retrieval)
distractorClues.gf: Wrong if need instant retrieval -> use Glacier Instant
distractorClues.gda: Wrong if data accessed frequently -> use Standard
distractorClues.it: Wrong if access pattern is known and steady -> use the specific tier (cheaper)

database-services.title: Amazon RDS vs Amazon Aurora vs Amazon DynamoDB vs Amazon Redshift vs Amazon ElastiCache vs Amazon DocumentDB
database-services.subtitle: Compare managed database services: relational, NoSQL, analytics, caching, and document databases

values: MySQL, PostgreSQL, MariaDB, Oracle, SQL Server
Vertical + up to 15 Read Replicas + Global

studyAids.mnemonic: RAPS-DI: R(elational=RDS/Aurora) + A(nalytic=Redshift) + P(ortable-doc=DocumentDB) + S(imple KV=DynamoDB) + D(istributed cache=ElastiCache) + I(ntelligent=Aurora Serverless)
studyAids.studyTips: RDS for managed SQL. Aurora for cloud-native high-perf SQL. DynamoDB for NoSQL (most tested). Redshift for analytics/OLAP. ElastiCache for caching. DocumentDB for MongoDB.

examKeywords.rds: Managed SQL, MySQL/Pg/Oracle, Read Replicas, Multi-AZ, Snapshots
examKeywords.aurora: 5x MySQL, 3x Pg, Auto-scaling, Global Database, Serverless
examKeywords.dynamodb: NoSQL, Single-digit ms, On-demand, DAX, Global Tables
examKeywords.redshift: OLAP, Columnar, BI, Analytics, Concurrency Scaling
examKeywords.docdb: MongoDB compatible, Document, JSON, BSON, Read Replicas

distractorClues.rds: Wrong if need cloud-native auto-scaling storage -> use Aurora. Wrong for NoSQL -> use DynamoDB
distractorClues.aurora: Wrong if not MySQL/PostgreSQL -> use RDS for other engines. Wrong for NoSQL -> use DynamoDB
distractorClues.dynamodb: Wrong for complex SQL/joins -> use Aurora/RDS
distractorClues.redshift: Wrong for OLTP/transactional workloads -> use Aurora/RDS
distractorClues.elasticache: Wrong for persistent primary database -> use RDS/DynamoDB
distractorClues.docdb: Wrong for SQL queries -> use Aurora/RDS

cache-services.title: ElastiCache Redis vs ElastiCache Memcached vs DAX (DynamoDB Accelerator)
cache-services.subtitle: Compare caching options: in-memory data stores and DynamoDB acceleration

values: Strings, lists, sets, hashes, sorted sets
Complex data types, pub/sub, sessions, leaderboards
"Need data structures or persistence in cache"

studyAids.mnemonic: RMD: R(edis=data structures, persistence) + M(emcached=simple fast cache) + D(AX=DynamoDB accelerator). Remember: Redis is Rich, Memcached is Minimal, DAX is DynamoDB-specific.
studyAids.studyTips: Redis supports data structures and persistence. Memcached is simpler with higher raw throughput. DAX is specifically for DynamoDB acceleration (microsecond reads).

examKeywords.redis: Data structures, Persistence, Pub/Sub, Sorted sets, Multi-AZ, Cluster
examKeywords.memcached: Simple key-value, Multi-threaded, Auto-sharding, No persistence, High throughput
examKeywords.dax: DynamoDB, Microsecond, Read acceleration, Compatible, Write-through

distractorClues.redis: Wrong if simple key-value cache is sufficient -> use Memcached (simpler, cheaper)
distractorClues.memcached: Wrong if need persistence or data structures -> use Redis
distractorClues.dax: Wrong if not using DynamoDB -> use ElastiCache Redis/Memcached

network-security.title: Security Groups vs NACLs vs AWS WAF vs AWS Shield
network-security.subtitle: Compare network security controls at different layers of the stack

values: Rules evaluated in order (lowest number first)
DDoS protection for all AWS customers

studyAids.mnemonic: SNW-S: S(ecurity Groups=instance level) + N(ACLs=subnet level) + W(AF=web app layer 7) + S(hield=DDoS). Security Groups are stateful, NACLs are stateless.
studyAids.studyTips: SG = stateful, instance-level, allow only. NACL = stateless, subnet-level, allow+deny, numbered rules. WAF = web app protection (SQLi, XSS). Shield Standard = free DDoS. Shield Advanced = $3000/mo.

examKeywords.sg: Stateful, Instance level, ENI, Allow only, Inbound/Outbound
examKeywords.nacl: Stateless, Subnet level, Allow+Deny, Numbered rules, Default deny
examKeywords.waf: Layer 7, Web ACL, SQL injection, XSS, Rate limiting, Bot control

distractorClues.sg: Wrong for subnet-level filtering -> use NACLs. Wrong for web app attacks -> use WAF
distractorClues.nacl: Wrong for instance-level control -> use Security Groups
distractorClues.waf: Wrong for network-level DDoS -> use Shield. Wrong for instance traffic -> use SG
distractorClues.shield: Wrong for application-layer attacks (SQLi) -> use WAF. Wrong for instance access -> use SG

vpc-components.title: VPC vs Subnet vs Route Table vs Internet Gateway vs NAT Gateway vs VPC Peering
vpc-components.subtitle: Compare VPC networking components and their roles in cloud network architecture

values: Isolated virtual network (like a data center)
Segment IP range into public/private zones
Connect two VPCs (same or different account)

studyAids.mnemonic: VPS-RIN: V(PC=network) + P(eering=connect VPCs) + S(ubnet=segment) + R(oute Table=direction) + I(nternet GW=public internet) + N(AT GW=private internet)
studyAids.studyTips: VPC is foundational. Public subnet = has IGW route. Private subnet = no direct internet. NAT Gateway for outbound-only private internet. Route Tables define traffic flow.

examKeywords.vpc: Isolated network, CIDR block, Regional, IPv4/IPv6
examKeywords.rt: Routes, Associated, Main route table, Propagation
examKeywords.igw: Internet access, Public subnet, Regional, No bandwidth limit
examKeywords.nat: Outbound only, Private subnet, AZ-scoped, Hourly + data charge

distractorClues.vpc: Wrong when asking about subnets, routing, or gateways -> use specific component
distractorClues.subnet: Wrong for routing -> use Route Table. Wrong for internet access -> use IGW/NAT
distractorClues.rt: Wrong for access control -> use Security Groups or NACLs
distractorClues.igw: Wrong for private subnet outbound -> use NAT Gateway. Wrong for VPC-to-VPC -> use Peering
distractorClues.nat: Wrong for inbound internet access -> use IGW + public subnet or ALB
distractorClues.peering: Wrong for internet access -> use IGW. Wrong for transitive routing -> use Transit Gateway

load-balancers.title: ALB vs NLB vs CLB vs GWLB
load-balancers.subtitle: Compare load balancer types by layer, protocols, and use cases

studyAids.mnemonic: ANG-L: A(LB=Layer 7 web) + N(LB=Layer 4 fast) + G(WLB=gateway) + L(egacy=CLB). Always Need Good Load balancing.
studyAids.studyTips: ALB = web apps, path-based routing, Layer 7. NLB = high throughput, Layer 4, ultra-low latency. CLB = legacy (avoid for new). GWLB = gateway load balancing for appliances.

examKeywords.alb: Layer 7, HTTP/HTTPS, Path routing, Host routing, Lambda target
examKeywords.nlb: Layer 4, TCP/UDP, High throughput, Ultra-low latency, Static IP
examKeywords.clb: Layer 4/7, Legacy, EC2-Classic, Avoid for new apps
examKeywords.gwlb: Gateway, Third-party appliances, Transparent, GENEVE protocol

distractorClues.alb: Wrong for ultra-low latency or raw TCP/UDP -> use NLB
distractorClues.nlb: Wrong for path-based routing -> use ALB
distractorClues.clb: Wrong for new applications -> use ALB or NLB (CLB is legacy)
distractorClues.gwlb: Wrong for web app load balancing -> use ALB

dns-routing.title: Route 53 Routing Policies: Simple vs Weighted vs Latency vs Failover vs Geolocation vs Multi-Value
dns-routing.subtitle: Compare DNS routing strategies for different traffic management needs

values: Load balancing with client-side random selection

studyAids.mnemonic: SWLF-GM: S(imple) + W(eighted=A/B test) + L(atency=fastest) + F(ailover=DR) + G(eolocation=location) + M(ulti-Value=multiple). Some Websites Load Fast, Go Multi.
studyAids.studyTips: Simple = basic. Weighted = A/B testing. Latency = fastest region. Failover = DR. Geolocation = location-based. Multi-Value = multiple records. Know when to use each.

examKeywords.simple: Single record, Round robin, Basic, No health check needed
examKeywords.weighted: Percentage, A/B testing, Gradual rollout, Weight parameter
examKeywords.latency: Lowest latency, Regional speed, Global apps
examKeywords.failover: Active-passive, DR, Health check required, Primary/Secondary
examKeywords.geo: User location, Country, Compliance, Data residency
examKeywords.mv: Multiple values, Client selection, Health check optional, Up to 8

distractorClues.simple: Wrong if need traffic distribution -> use Weighted or Latency
distractorClues.weighted: Wrong if need lowest latency -> use Latency. Wrong for DR -> use Failover
distractorClues.latency: Wrong if need location-based routing -> use Geolocation
distractorClues.failover: Wrong for active-active -> use Latency or Weighted
distractorClues.geo: Wrong if need fastest response -> use Latency (geo may not route to nearest)
distractorClues.mv: Wrong for guaranteed routing logic -> use specific policy (Latency, Failover, etc.)

hybrid-connectivity.title: Site-to-Site VPN vs AWS Direct Connect vs AWS Outposts vs AWS Snow Family
hybrid-connectivity.subtitle: Compare hybrid connectivity options: connecting on-premises to AWS

values: Encrypted IPsec tunnel over public internet

studyAids.mnemonic: VDOS: V(PN=quick encrypted) + D(irect Connect=consistent fast) + O(utposts=on-prem AWS) + S(now=physical transfer). Very Dependable On-Site.
studyAids.studyTips: VPN = quick, encrypted, internet. Direct Connect = dedicated, consistent, expensive. Outposts = AWS on-prem. Snow = physical data transfer for massive datasets.

examKeywords.vpn: IPsec, Quick setup, Variable latency, Encrypted, Internet
examKeywords.dx: Dedicated, Consistent latency, 1-100 Gbps, Private, Expensive
examKeywords.outposts: On-premises, Low latency, EC2/EKS locally, Hybrid
examKeywords.snow: Petabyte, Offline, Snowball Edge, Snowmobile, Physical transfer

distractorClues.vpn: Wrong if consistent latency required -> use Direct Connect
distractorClues.dx: Wrong if quick setup needed -> use VPN. Wrong for massive data migration -> use Snow
distractorClues.outposts: Wrong for just network connectivity -> use VPN or Direct Connect
distractorClues.snow: Wrong for small data transfers -> use DataSync or VPN

edge-services.title: Amazon CloudFront vs AWS Global Accelerator vs AWS Local Zones vs AWS Wavelength
edge-services.subtitle: Compare edge services for content delivery, network performance, and ultra-low latency

values: Network performance, fixed IP entry points
Bring AWS closer to end users in specific metros
Static content, API caching, media delivery
Gaming, IoT, non-HTTP apps needing low latency

studyAids.mnemonic: CGL-W: C(loudFront=CDN/content) + G(lobal Accelerator=network L4) + L(ocal Zones=metro proximity) + W(avelength=5G ultra-low). Content Goes Locally With speed.
studyAids.studyTips: CloudFront = CDN for HTTP content. Global Accelerator = network L4 performance (not content caching). Local Zones = metro-specific AWS infrastructure. Wavelength = 5G carrier edge.

examKeywords.cf: CDN, Edge locations, Cache, HTTP/HTTPS, Distribution, Origin
examKeywords.ga: Anycast IPs, Layer 4, TCP/UDP, Network performance, Fixed IPs
examKeywords.lz: Metro area, Proximity, Low latency, Specific city
examKeywords.wl: 5G, Carrier edge, Ultra-low latency, ML inference, Gaming

distractorClues.cf: Wrong for non-HTTP or no caching needed -> use Global Accelerator
distractorClues.ga: Wrong for content caching -> use CloudFront. Wrong for HTTP optimization -> use CloudFront
distractorClues.lz: Wrong for global content delivery -> use CloudFront. Wrong for ultra-low 5G -> use Wavelength
distractorClues.wl: Wrong if no 5G carrier partnership -> use Local Zones or CloudFront

iam-components.title: IAM Users vs Groups vs Roles vs Policies vs AWS SSO vs Amazon Cognito
iam-components.subtitle: Compare IAM components for identity and access management

values: Centralized access for multiple AWS accounts
Organize users, apply permissions at group level

studyAids.mnemonic: UGRP-SC: U(sers=people) + G(roups=organize users) + R(oles=temporary) + P(olicies=permissions JSON) + S(SO=multi-account) + C(ognito=app users). Users Get Roles, Policies Secure Control.
studyAids.studyTips: IAM is the #1 security topic. Users = long-term, Roles = temporary. Always use Roles for services (EC2, Lambda). Policies define permissions (JSON). SSO for multi-account. Cognito for app authentication.

examKeywords.user: Long-term, Access key, Password, MFA, Individual
examKeywords.group: Collection of users, Permissions attached, Best practice
examKeywords.role: Temporary, STS, AssumeRole, Cross-account, Service role
examKeywords.sso: IAM Identity Center, Multi-account, Federation, SAML
examKeywords.cognito: User pools, Identity pools, OAuth, JWT, Web/mobile sign-in

distractorClues.user: Wrong for services -> use IAM Role. Wrong for app users -> use Cognito
distractorClues.group: Wrong for services -> use Roles. Not a substitute for least-privilege
distractorClues.role: Wrong for long-term human access -> use User
distractorClues.policy: Wrong as an identity -> it defines permissions, not identity
distractorClues.sso: Wrong for app user authentication -> use Cognito
distractorClues.cognito: Wrong for internal AWS access -> use IAM Users/Roles/SSO

encryption-services.title: AWS KMS vs AWS CloudHSM vs AWS Secrets Manager vs AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store vs ACM
encryption-services.subtitle: Compare encryption, key management, secrets, and certificate services

values: Text secrets (passwords, API keys, connection strings)
Yes (public certs free, private certs paid)
Rotating database credentials and API keys

studyAids.mnemonic: KSC-PA: K(MS=encryption keys) + S(ecrets Manager=passwords) + C(loudHSM=compliance HSM) + P(arameter Store=config) + A(CM=certificates). Keys Secure Configurations, Passwords Authenticated.
studyAids.studyTips: KMS = encryption keys (most common). CloudHSM = dedicated HSM for compliance. Secrets Manager = rotate secrets automatically. Parameter Store = configuration. ACM = SSL/TLS certificates.

examKeywords.kms: CMK, Data key, Envelope encryption, SSE-KMS, Rotation
examKeywords.sm: Rotate, Database password, API key, RDS/Aurora integration
examKeywords.ps: Parameter, SecureString, Hierarchy, Free tier, Configuration
examKeywords.acm: SSL/TLS, Certificate, Renewal, ALB/CloudFront, Private CA

distractorClues.kms: Wrong if need FIPS Level 3 dedicated HSM -> use CloudHSM
distractorClues.hsm: Wrong for general encryption -> use KMS (simpler, cheaper)
distractorClues.sm: Wrong for configuration parameters -> use Parameter Store. Wrong for encryption keys -> use KMS
distractorClues.ps: Wrong for secrets requiring rotation -> use Secrets Manager
distractorClues.acm: Wrong for managing encryption keys -> use KMS. Wrong for API keys -> use Secrets Manager

security-monitoring.title: Amazon GuardDuty vs Amazon Inspector vs AWS Security Hub vs Amazon Detective vs Amazon Macie
security-monitoring.subtitle: Compare AWS security monitoring, scanning, and threat detection services

values: VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, DNS logs
CloudTrail, VPC Flow Logs, GuardDuty findings

studyAids.mnemonic: GISDM: G(uardDuty=threat detection) + I(nspector=vulnerability scan) + S(ecurity Hub=aggregation) + D(etective=investigation) + M(acie=PII protection). Guard Inspect, Secure, Detect, Manage.
studyAids.studyTips: GuardDuty = threat detection. Inspector = vulnerability scanning. Security Hub = central dashboard. Detective = investigation (root cause). Macie = PII/S3 data protection.

examKeywords.gd: Threat detection, ML-based, CloudTrail + VPC Flow Logs, Finding
examKeywords.insp: Vulnerability scan, CVE, EC2, ECR, Container scanning
examKeywords.det: Investigation, Timeline, Graph, Root cause, Correlation
examKeywords.macie: PII, S3, Data classification, ML, Sensitive data

distractorClues.gd: Wrong for vulnerability scanning -> use Inspector. Wrong for posture dashboard -> use Security Hub
distractorClues.insp: Wrong for threat detection -> use GuardDuty
distractorClues.sh: Wrong for threat detection -> use GuardDuty. Wrong for investigation -> use Detective
distractorClues.det: Wrong for real-time threat detection -> use GuardDuty
distractorClues.macie: Wrong for general threat detection -> use GuardDuty. Wrong for vulnerability scan -> use Inspector

compliance-governance.title: AWS CloudTrail vs AWS Config vs AWS Artifact vs AWS Audit Manager vs IAM Access Analyzer
compliance-governance.subtitle: Compare governance, compliance, auditing, and access analysis services

values: WHO did WHAT, WHEN? (API audit log)
WHAT changed, HOW is it configured? (resource compliance)
WHERE are AWS compliance reports? (download agreements)
HOW do we audit for compliance? (automated evidence)
WHO has access to WHAT resources? (permission analysis)
All API calls in the account/region
Free (first copy per region) + data events

studyAids.mnemonic: CCAA-A: C(loudTrail=audit log) + C(onfig=resource tracking) + A(rtifact=compliance docs) + A(udit Manager=compliance audit) + A(ccess Analyzer=permissions). Cloud Config Audits All Access.
studyAids.studyTips: CloudTrail = API audit log (WHO did WHAT). Config = resource compliance (WHAT changed). Artifact = compliance docs download. Audit Manager = automated compliance auditing. Access Analyzer = permission analysis.

examKeywords.ct: API calls, Audit, Log, Management/Data events, S3 delivery
examKeywords.cfg: Configuration, Compliance, Rules, Resource tracking, Timeline
examKeywords.art: Compliance, Reports, Agreements, Security artifacts, PDF
examKeywords.am: Audit, Evidence collection, Frameworks (CIS, PCI), Continuous
examKeywords.aa: Permissions, Unused permissions, Cross-account, S3 bucket policy, IAM policy

distractorClues.ct: Wrong for resource configuration tracking -> use Config
distractorClues.cfg: Wrong for API call audit -> use CloudTrail
distractorClues.art: Wrong for auditing -> use Audit Manager. Wrong for monitoring -> use CloudTrail/Config
distractorClues.am: Wrong for real-time monitoring -> use GuardDuty. Wrong for API logs -> use CloudTrail
distractorClues.aa: Wrong for compliance evidence collection -> use Audit Manager. Wrong for threat detection -> use GuardDuty

monitoring-logging.title: Amazon CloudWatch vs AWS CloudTrail vs AWS X-Ray vs VPC Flow Logs
monitoring-logging.subtitle: Compare observability services: metrics, logs, tracing, and network logging

values: Monitor metrics, collect logs, set alarms
Capture network traffic info for VPC
Yes (via Athena or CloudWatch Logs Insights with export)
Free (S3 export free, VPC Flow Logs standard free)

studyAids.mnemonic: CCTX: C(loudWatch=metrics/alarms) + C(loudTrail=API audit) + T(race=X-Ray) + eXamine(VPC Flow Logs). CloudWatch Catches Things, X-Ray Traces eXtensions.
studyAids.studyTips: CloudWatch = metrics + logs + alarms. CloudTrail = API audit (WHO did WHAT). X-Ray = distributed tracing (request flow). VPC Flow Logs = network traffic analysis.

examKeywords.cw: Metrics, Logs, Alarms, Dashboards, Events, Auto Scaling trigger
examKeywords.ct: API calls, Audit, Management events, Data events, Organizations
examKeywords.xr: Trace, Distributed, Segments, Annotations, Service Map
examKeywords.vpcfl: Network, VPC, ENI, Subnet, Accept/Reject, Traffic analysis

distractorClues.cw: Wrong for API audit -> use CloudTrail. Wrong for distributed tracing -> use X-Ray
distractorClues.ct: Wrong for application metrics -> use CloudWatch. Wrong for tracing -> use X-Ray
distractorClues.xr: Wrong for API audit -> use CloudTrail. Wrong for infrastructure metrics -> use CloudWatch
distractorClues.vpcfl: Wrong for API audit -> use CloudTrail. Wrong for app performance -> use CloudWatch/X-Ray

iac-deployment.title: CloudFormation vs AWS CDK vs AWS SAM vs Elastic Beanstalk vs OpsWorks
iac-deployment.subtitle: Compare infrastructure as code and deployment services

values: Infrastructure as code, full resource provisioning
Developers who prefer code over YAML
Quick web app deployment, no IaC needed

studyAids.mnemonic: CCSE-O: C(loudFormation=IaC YAML/JSON) + C(DK=code-based IaC) + S(AM=serverless) + E(B=PaaS quick deploy) + O(psWorks=Chef config mgmt). Cloud Code Simplifies Everything, Ops Optional.
studyAids.studyTips: CloudFormation = IaC king (YAML/JSON). CDK = CloudFormation in code (TypeScript/Python). SAM = serverless simplified CF. Elastic Beanstalk = PaaS (quick deploy). OpsWorks = Chef-based config management.

examKeywords.cf: YAML, JSON, Stack, ChangeSet, Drift detection, Nested stacks
examKeywords.cdk: TypeScript, Python, Construct, Synth to CloudFormation, Programmatic
examKeywords.sam: Serverless, Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, sam deploy
examKeywords.eb: PaaS, Upload code, Environments, Auto-scaling, Quick deploy
examKeywords.ops: Chef, Recipes, Layers, Stacks, Configuration management

distractorClues.cf: Wrong if prefer writing code over YAML -> use CDK
distractorClues.cdk: Wrong if YAML/JSON is fine -> use CloudFormation (simpler)
distractorClues.sam: Wrong for non-serverless infrastructure -> use CloudFormation
distractorClues.eb: Wrong if need full infrastructure control -> use CloudFormation. Wrong for containers -> use ECS
distractorClues.ops: Wrong for simple IaC -> use CloudFormation. Chef knowledge required

cost-management.title: AWS Cost Explorer vs AWS Budgets vs AWS Trusted Advisor vs AWS Pricing Calculator
cost-management.subtitle: Compare cost analysis, alerting, optimization, and estimation services

values: Set cost/alert thresholds and get notified

studyAids.mnemonic: CBTP: C(ost Explorer=analyze) + B(udgets=alerts) + T(rusted Advisor=recommendations) + P(ricing Calculator=estimate). Count Budgets To Pennies.
studyAids.studyTips: Cost Explorer = analyze spending trends. Budgets = alerts on thresholds. Trusted Advisor = optimization recommendations. Pricing Calculator = estimate before deploying.

examKeywords.ce: Analyze, Forecast, Trends, Filters, Groups, 12 months
examKeywords.bud: Alerts, Budget, Threshold, SNS notification, Action
examKeywords.ta: Best practices, Optimization, Security, Performance, Limits
examKeywords.pc: Estimate, Before deploy, Workload calculator, TCO

distractorClues.ce: Wrong for alerts -> use Budgets. Wrong for pre-deployment -> use Pricing Calculator
distractorClues.bud: Wrong for analysis -> use Cost Explorer. Wrong for optimization -> use Trusted Advisor
distractorClues.ta: Wrong for cost estimation -> use Pricing Calculator. Wrong for alerts -> use Budgets
distractorClues.pc: Wrong for actual spending analysis -> use Cost Explorer

support-plans.title: AWS Support Plans: Developer vs Business vs Enterprise On-Ramp vs Enterprise
support-plans.subtitle: Compare AWS support plans: response times, features, and costs

studyAids.mnemonic: DBEE: D(eveloper=free) + B(usiness=$29+3%) + E(nterprise On-Ramp=$100+10%) + E(nterprise=$15K+). Developer Builds, Business Expands, Enterprise Elevates.
studyAids.studyTips: Developer = free, limited. Business = $29/mo, all Trusted Advisor, 24/7. Enterprise On-Ramp = growing businesses. Enterprise = TAM, concierge, custom.

examKeywords.dev: Free, Limited, 7 Trusted Advisor checks, Business hours
examKeywords.biz: $29, 24/7, All Trusted Advisor, 15 min critical response
examKeywords.eor: $100 min, 24/7, Guidance, All checks, On-Ramp to Enterprise
examKeywords.ent: $15K min, TAM, Concierge, Custom, Proactive guidance

distractorClues.dev: Wrong for production -> use Business or Enterprise
distractorClues.biz: Wrong if need TAM -> use Enterprise
distractorClues.eor: Wrong for free support -> use Developer. Wrong if need TAM -> use Enterprise
distractorClues.ent: Wrong for small/medium budget -> use Business or Enterprise On-Ramp

data-analytics.title: Amazon Athena vs Amazon Redshift vs Amazon EMR vs AWS Glue vs AWS Lake Formation
data-analytics.subtitle: Compare data analytics, warehousing, processing, and governance services

values: Serverless SQL query on S3 data
Govern and share data lake securely
"SQL query on S3 without moving data"

studyAids.mnemonic: AREGL: A(thena=SQL on S3) + R(edshift=data warehouse) + E(MR=Hadoop/Spark) + G(lue=ETL/catalog) + L(ake Formation=governance). Analyze, Report, Extract, Govern, Label.
studyAids.studyTips: Athena = serverless SQL on S3. Redshift = data warehouse (OLAP). EMR = big data (Spark/Hadoop). Glue = ETL + data catalog. Lake Formation = data lake governance.

examKeywords.athena: Serverless SQL, S3, Presto, No infrastructure, Pay per query
examKeywords.rs: Data warehouse, OLAP, Columnar, Concurrency Scaling, BI
examKeywords.emr: Hadoop, Spark, Big data, Managed cluster, Processing
examKeywords.glue: ETL, Data Catalog, Crawler, PySpark, Serverless
examKeywords.lf: Data lake, Governance, Fine-grained access, Sharing, Centralized

distractorClues.athena: Wrong for persistent data warehouse -> use Redshift. Wrong for ETL -> use Glue
distractorClues.rs: Wrong for ad-hoc S3 queries -> use Athena. Wrong for big data processing -> use EMR
distractorClues.emr: Wrong for serverless or simple SQL -> use Athena. Wrong for ETL -> use Glue
distractorClues.glue: Wrong for SQL queries -> use Athena. Wrong for data warehouse -> use Redshift
distractorClues.lf: Wrong for data processing -> use Glue or EMR. Wrong for SQL -> use Athena

streaming-services.title: Kinesis Data Streams vs Kinesis Data Firehose vs Kinesis Data Analytics vs Amazon MSK
streaming-services.subtitle: Compare real-time data streaming services

studyAids.mnemonic: KFAM: K(inesis Streams=custom processing) + F(irehose=delivery) + A(nalytics=SQL on streams) + M(SK=Kafka). Kinesis For All Messages.
studyAids.studyTips: Kinesis Data Streams = custom processing (shard-based). Firehose = deliver to destinations (serverless). Data Analytics = real-time SQL. MSK = managed Kafka.

examKeywords.kds: Shards, Real-time, Custom consumers, Partition key, Provisioned
examKeywords.kdf: Delivery, S3, Redshift, Serverless, Auto-scaling, No coding
examKeywords.kda: SQL, Real-time analytics, Flink, Window functions
examKeywords.msk: Apache Kafka, Managed, Brokers, Topics, Partitions, ZooKeeper

distractorClues.kds: Wrong if just delivering to S3 -> use Firehose (simpler). Wrong for SQL -> use Data Analytics
distractorClues.kdf: Wrong if need custom processing logic -> use Data Streams. Wrong for SQL -> use Data Analytics
distractorClues.kda: Wrong if need to deliver to storage -> use Firehose. Wrong for Kafka -> use MSK
distractorClues.msk: Wrong if not using Kafka -> use Kinesis Data Streams

bi-visualization.title: Amazon QuickSight vs OpenSearch Dashboards
bi-visualization.subtitle: Compare BI visualization and dashboarding services

values: 40+ data sources (RDS, Redshift, S3, Athena, etc.)

studyAids.mnemonic: QO: Q(uickSight=BI dashboards) + O(penSearch Dashboards=log/search viz). Quick Overview.
studyAids.studyTips: QuickSight = BI for business users (dashboards, reports, ad-hoc analysis). OpenSearch Dashboards = log/search visualization (Kibana-like).

examKeywords.qs: BI, Dashboard, SPICE, Embedding, Ad-hoc analysis, Business
examKeywords.osd: Log visualization, Search analytics, Kibana-like, OpenSearch

distractorClues.qs: Wrong for log visualization -> use OpenSearch Dashboards
distractorClues.osd: Wrong for general BI -> use QuickSight

messaging-services.title: SQS Standard vs SQS FIFO vs Amazon SNS vs Amazon MQ
messaging-services.subtitle: Compare messaging patterns: queues, pub/sub, and legacy message brokers

studyAids.mnemonic: SSF-M: S(QS Standard=decouple) + S(QS FIFO=ordered) + F(an-out=SNS) + M(Q=legacy broker). Simple Services Fan-out Messages.
studyAids.studyTips: SQS = queue (decouple). SQS FIFO = ordered queue. SNS = pub/sub (fan-out). Amazon MQ = legacy message broker. SNS + SQS = fan-out to queues.

examKeywords.sqs-s: Queue, Decouple, At-least-once, Unlimited throughput, Standard
examKeywords.sqs-f: FIFO, Ordered, Exactly-once, Dedup, Message group, 300 TPS
examKeywords.mq: ActiveMQ, RabbitMQ, Legacy, AMQP, Migration, Compatible

distractorClues.sqs-s: Wrong if ordering required -> use SQS FIFO
distractorClues.sqs-f: Wrong if need fan-out -> use SNS. Wrong if max throughput needed -> use SQS Standard
distractorClues.sns: Wrong for point-to-point -> use SQS. Wrong for ordered delivery -> use SQS FIFO
distractorClues.mq: Wrong for new applications -> use SQS/SNS. Only for legacy migration

event-services.title: Amazon EventBridge vs CloudWatch Events vs Amazon SNS (event patterns)
event-services.subtitle: Compare event routing and notification services

values: Event bus for routing events between services
Schedule and react to AWS events
Simple scheduled events, AWS service events

studyAids.mnemonic: ECS: E(ventBridge=event routing + SaaS) + C(loudWatch Events=scheduled/AWS events) + S(NS=push notifications). Events Connect Services.
studyAids.studyTips: EventBridge is the newer, more powerful replacement for CloudWatch Events. It adds SaaS integration, custom event buses, and content filtering. SNS is for push notifications.

examKeywords.eb: Event bus, Content filtering, SaaS integration, Partner events, Archive/Replay
examKeywords.cwe: Default bus, Schedule, AWS events, Rule-based, Legacy
examKeywords.sns: Topic, Subscription, Email, SMS, Push, Fan-out

distractorClues.eb: Wrong for simple scheduled tasks -> CloudWatch Events still works. Wrong for push notifications -> use SNS
distractorClues.cwe: Wrong for SaaS integration -> use EventBridge. Wrong for notifications -> use SNS
distractorClues.sns: Wrong for event routing between services -> use EventBridge

workflow-services.title: AWS Step Functions Standard vs Express vs Amazon SWF
workflow-services.subtitle: Compare workflow orchestration services

studyAids.mnemonic: SSE: S(tandard=long durable) + S(Express=high throughput short) + (old) S(WF=decider legacy). Standard Solid, Express Swift.
studyAids.studyTips: Standard = long-running (up to 1 year), durable. Express = high-throughput, short (5 min). SWF = legacy decider pattern. Step Functions is the modern choice.

examKeywords.sf-s: 1 year, Durable, Execution history, Higher cost per transition
examKeywords.sf-e: 5 minutes, High throughput, Low cost, No execution history
examKeywords.swf: Decider, Workers, Legacy, Event-driven, Amazon Simple Workflow

distractorClues.sf-s: Wrong for high-throughput short workflows -> use Express (cheaper per transition)
distractorClues.sf-e: Wrong for long-running workflows -> use Standard. Wrong if execution history needed -> use Standard
distractorClues.swf: Wrong for new applications -> use Step Functions (modern replacement)

ml-platform.title: SageMaker Canvas vs SageMaker Studio vs SageMaker Pipelines vs SageMaker JumpStart
ml-platform.subtitle: Compare Amazon SageMaker tools for different ML development needs

values: No-code ML model building and deployment
Full IDE for ML development, training, tuning
Pre-trained models, quick experiment, ML demos
"Business analyst wants to build ML without code"

studyAids.mnemonic: CSJ-P: C(anvas=no-code for analysts) + S(tudio=IDE for scientists) + J(umpStart=pre-trained models) + P(ipeelines=MLOps). Code Scientists Jump to Pipelines.
studyAids.studyTips: Canvas = no-code ML for business users. Studio = full IDE for data scientists. Pipelines = MLOps CI/CD. JumpStart = pre-trained models, quick start.

examKeywords.canvas: No-code, Visual, Business analyst, AutoML, Drag-and-drop
examKeywords.studio: IDE, Notebooks, Training, Tuning, Debugger, Profiler
examKeywords.pipes: CI/CD, Pipeline, Model registry, Auto-scaling, MLOps
examKeywords.js: Pre-trained, One-click, Foundation models, Deploy, Experiment

distractorClues.canvas: Wrong if need custom code/models -> use Studio
distractorClues.studio: Wrong for no-code users -> use Canvas. Wrong for quick deploy -> use JumpStart
distractorClues.pipes: Wrong for model building -> use Studio. Only for orchestration
distractorClues.js: Wrong for custom model training -> use Studio

genai-services.title: Amazon Bedrock vs Amazon Q vs PartyRock vs SageMaker JumpStart (GenAI)
genai-services.subtitle: Compare AWS generative AI services for building AI applications

values: Access foundation models via API, build GenAI apps
AI assistant for business and developers
Pre-trained models for quick ML deployment
"Build GenAI app with FM API access"

studyAids.mnemonic: BQP-J: B(edrock=FM API) + Q(=AI assistant) + P(artyRock=free playground) + J(umpStart=pre-trained). Build Quality Products with JumpStart.
studyAids.studyTips: Bedrock = core GenAI service (FM API, agents, RAG, guardrails). Q = AI assistant (Business for users, Developer for code). PartyRock = free experiment. JumpStart = pre-trained models.

examKeywords.bedrock: Foundation models, Agents, Knowledge Bases, Guardrails, RAG, Fine-tuning
examKeywords.q: AI assistant, Business, Developer, Code, Enterprise data, Conversation
examKeywords.js: Pre-trained, Deploy, ML models, Quick start, Gallery

distractorClues.bedrock: Wrong for free experiment -> use PartyRock. Wrong for AI assistant -> use Q
distractorClues.q: Wrong for building custom GenAI apps -> use Bedrock
distractorClues.party: Wrong for production apps -> use Bedrock
distractorClues.js: Wrong for no-code GenAI experiment -> use PartyRock

bedrock-models.title: Claude vs Amazon Titan vs Meta Llama vs Mistral AI vs Cohere vs Stability AI
bedrock-models.subtitle: Compare foundation models available in Amazon Bedrock

values: Complex reasoning, coding, analysis, long context

studyAids.mnemonic: CTLM-CS: C(laude=reasoning/coding) + T(itan=Amazon general) + L(lama=open-source) + M(istral=efficient) + C(ohere=text/embeddings) + S(tability=images). Claude Thinks, Llama Learns, Mistral Moves, Cohere Creates.
studyAids.studyTips: Claude = best for reasoning/coding. Titan = Amazon general. Llama = open-source. Mistral = efficient. Cohere = text/embeddings. Stability = images.

examKeywords.claude: Anthropic, Reasoning, Coding, Analysis, Long context, Safety

distractorClues.claude: Wrong if need image generation -> use Stability AI
distractorClues.titan: Wrong if need best-in-class reasoning -> use Claude
distractorClues.llama: Wrong if need managed training/fine-tuning with support -> use Claude or Titan
distractorClues.mistral: Wrong if need image generation -> use Stability AI
distractorClues.cohere: Wrong for image generation -> use Stability AI
distractorClues.stability: Wrong for text/reasoning tasks -> use Claude, Titan, or Llama

rag-approaches.title: Prompt Engineering vs RAG (Knowledge Bases) vs Fine-Tuning vs Continued Pre-Training
rag-approaches.subtitle: Compare approaches for customizing foundation models, from cheapest to most expensive

studyAids.mnemonic: PRFC: P(rompt=cheapest) + R(AG=add knowledge) + F(ine-tune=domain expertise) + C(ontinued Pre-Training=full control). Prompting, Retrieval, Fine-tuning, Continued.
studyAids.studyTips: Prompt engineering = cheapest, no data needed. RAG = add knowledge via retrieval (Bedrock KB). Fine-tuning = change model weights for domain expertise. Continued pre-training = most expensive, full domain control.

examKeywords.prompt: Cheapest, No data, Real-time, Prompt engineering, Zero/few-shot
examKeywords.rag: Retrieval, Knowledge Bases, Company data, Bedrock KB, Grounding
examKeywords.ft: Fine-tune, Domain expertise, Model weights, Training data, Bedrock Custom
examKeywords.cpt: Continued Pre-Training, Full control, Large corpus, SageMaker, Expensive

distractorClues.prompt: Wrong if model lacks knowledge -> use RAG or Fine-tuning
distractorClues.rag: Wrong if model behavior needs changing -> use Fine-tuning. Wrong if no documents available -> use Prompt
distractorClues.ft: Wrong if just need factual answers -> use RAG (cheaper, real-time). Wrong for quick iteration -> use Prompt
distractorClues.cpt: Wrong if fine-tuning would suffice -> use Fine-tuning (cheaper, faster)

ai-vision.title: Amazon Rekognition vs Amazon Textract vs Amazon Lookout for Vision
ai-vision.subtitle: Compare AWS AI vision services for image analysis, document processing, and defect detection

values: Extract text and data from documents
Labels, faces, celebrities, text in images, PPE detection
Structured text, forms, tables, key-value pairs
Content moderation, face detection, celebrity recognition
Document processing, forms automation, data extraction

studyAids.mnemonic: RTL: R(ekognition=image/video analysis) + T(extract=document text) + L(ookout for Vision=defect detection). Recognize Text, Locate defects.
studyAids.studyTips: Rekognition = image/video analysis (faces, labels, moderation). Textract = document OCR and form extraction. Lookout for Vision = manufacturing defect detection.

examKeywords.rekog: Image, Video, Face detection, Labels, PPE, Content moderation
examKeywords.textract: OCR, Forms, Tables, Documents, Invoices, Structured data
examKeywords.lookout: Defect, Anomaly, Manufacturing, Quality control, Custom model

distractorClues.rekog: Wrong for document text extraction -> use Textract. Wrong for manufacturing defects -> use Lookout for Vision
distractorClues.textract: Wrong for image classification/face detection -> use Rekognition
distractorClues.lookout: Wrong for general image analysis -> use Rekognition. Wrong for documents -> use Textract

ai-language.title: Amazon Comprehend vs Amazon Translate vs Amazon Polly vs Amazon Transcribe vs Amazon Lex
ai-language.subtitle: Compare AWS AI language services for text analysis, translation, speech, and chatbots

values: NLP: sentiment, entities, key phrases, language detection
Sentiment, entities, key phrases, language, PII
Analyze customer feedback, detect PII, extract entities

studyAids.mnemonic: CTPT-L: C(omprehend=analyze text) + T(ranslate=language) + P(olly=text-to-speech) + T(ranscribe=speech-to-text) + L(ex=chatbots). Comprehend Text, Produce Talk, Listen.
studyAids.studyTips: Comprehend = analyze text (NLP). Translate = language translation. Polly = TTS (text to speech). Transcribe = STT (speech to text). Lex = chatbots (uses the same tech as Alexa).

examKeywords.comp: Sentiment, Entities, Key phrases, Language detection, PII, NLP
examKeywords.trans: Translation, Localization, Language, Neural MT, Real-time
examKeywords.polly: TTS, Speech, Voices, SSML, Lifelike, Podcasts
examKeywords.tscribe: STT, Speech-to-text, Transcription, Timestamps, Speakers, Auto-language
examKeywords.lex: Chatbot, Alexa, Intents, Slots, Fulfillment, Voice+Text

distractorClues.comp: Wrong for translation -> use Translate. Wrong for speech -> use Transcribe
distractorClues.trans: Wrong for text analysis -> use Comprehend
distractorClues.lex: Wrong for simple text analysis -> use Comprehend. Wrong for just speech -> use Transcribe/Polly

ai-search.title: Amazon Kendra vs Amazon Personalize vs Amazon OpenSearch vs Bedrock Knowledge Bases
ai-search.subtitle: Compare AI-powered search, personalization, and knowledge services

values: RAG: add knowledge to foundation models

studyAids.mnemonic: KPOB: K(endra=enterprise search) + P(ersonalize=recommendations) + O(penSearch=search/analytics) + B(edrock KB=RAG for FMs). Knowledge Provides Optimal Business intelligence.
studyAids.studyTips: Kendra = enterprise search with NLP. Personalize = recommendations. OpenSearch = search/analytics (Elasticsearch). Bedrock KB = RAG for foundation models.

examKeywords.kendra: Enterprise search, NLP, Natural language, FAQ, Documents
examKeywords.personalize: Recommendations, Personalization, User behavior, Collaborative filtering
examKeywords.os: Search, Analytics, Elasticsearch, Logs, Dashboards, Vector search
examKeywords.kb: RAG, Knowledge Bases, Bedrock, Embeddings, Grounding, Retrieval

distractorClues.kendra: Wrong for log analytics -> use OpenSearch. Wrong for recommendations -> use Personalize
distractorClues.personalize: Wrong for search -> use OpenSearch or Kendra. Wrong for RAG -> use Bedrock KB
distractorClues.os: Wrong for enterprise NLP search -> use Kendra. Wrong for recommendations -> use Personalize
distractorClues.kb: Wrong for general search/analytics -> use OpenSearch. Wrong for recommendations -> use Personalize

responsible-ai.title: SageMaker Clarify vs Bedrock Guardrails vs Model Cards vs Augmented AI (A2I)
responsible-ai.subtitle: Compare responsible AI tools: fairness, safety, documentation, and human review

values: Filter harmful content from FM inputs/outputs
Bias in data/models, feature importance, SHAP values
Harmful content, PII, profanity, topic restrictions
N/A (it routes predictions to human reviewers)
Content filtering, topic blocking, word filters
Bias detection in ML models and data

studyAids.mnemonic: CGM-A: C(larify=bias detection) + G(uardrails=content safety) + M(odel Cards=documentation) + A(ugmented AI=human review). Clarify Guardrails, Model cards Are essential.
studyAids.studyTips: Clarify = detect bias, explain predictions. Guardrails = filter harmful FM content. Model Cards = document model info. A2I = human-in-the-loop review.

examKeywords.clarify: Bias, Explainability, SHAP, Feature importance, Fairness
examKeywords.guard: Content filter, Harmful, PII, Profanity, Topic, Bedrock
examKeywords.a2i: Human review, Human-in-the-loop, Augmented AI, Prediction review

distractorClues.clarify: Wrong for content filtering -> use Guardrails. Wrong for human review -> use A2I
distractorClues.guard: Wrong for bias detection -> use Clarify. Wrong for model documentation -> use Model Cards
distractorClues.mc: Wrong for bias detection -> use Clarify. Wrong for content filtering -> use Guardrails
distractorClues.a2i: Wrong for bias detection -> use Clarify. Wrong for content safety -> use Guardrails

migration-strategies.title: Rehost vs Replatform vs Refactor vs Repurchase vs Retire vs Retain
migration-strategies.subtitle: Compare the 6 Rs of migration strategy

values: Lift EC2 from VMware to AWS
Move to RDS/Aurora, use managed services

studyAids.mnemonic: 6-Rs: R(ehost=lift&shift) + R(eplatform=lift&reshape) + R(earchitect=redesign) + R(epurchase=SaaS) + R(etire=decommission) + R(etrain=keep). Remember: Run Right, Refactor Quickly.
studyAids.studyTips: The 6 Rs: Rehost (lift-shift), Replatform (lift-reshape), Refactor/Rearchitect, Repurchase (SaaS), Retire, Retain. Rehost = fastest/cheapest. Refactor = best long-term.

examKeywords.rehost: Lift and shift, Minimal changes, Fastest, MGN
examKeywords.replatform: Lift and reshape, Managed services, RDS, Aurora

distractorClues.rehost: Wrong if want to optimize -> use Replatform or Refactor
distractorClues.replatform: Wrong if need cloud-native redesign -> use Refactor
distractorClues.refactor: Wrong if need fastest/cheapest migration -> use Rehost
distractorClues.repurchase: Wrong if need custom solution -> use Refactor
distractorClues.retire: Wrong for critical applications -> use any of the other Rs
distractorClues.retain: Wrong if moving to cloud is the goal -> choose appropriate R

data-transfer.title: Snowball vs Snowball Edge vs Snowmobile vs AWS DataSync vs AWS Transfer Family
data-transfer.subtitle: Compare data transfer services for moving data into and out of AWS

values: "Need compute + storage at edge during transfer"

studyAids.mnemonic: SSDD-T: S(nowball=80TB) + S(nowball Edge=100TB+compute) + D(ataSync=continuous online) + D(eployment=Transfer Family=SFTP). Small Snow, Big Snowmobile, Daily DataSync.
studyAids.studyTips: Snowball = 80TB offline. Snowball Edge = 100TB + compute. Snowmobile = exabytes (truck). DataSync = continuous online sync. Transfer Family = SFTP/FTPS access to S3.

examKeywords.sbe: 100 TB, Compute, Storage, Edge, Offline, GPU option
examKeywords.snowmobile: Exabyte, Truck, Offline, Massive scale, One-time
examKeywords.transfer: SFTP, FTPS, FTP, Managed, S3 access, File protocols

distractorClues.snowball: Wrong if >80TB -> use Snowball Edge or Snowmobile. Wrong for continuous -> use DataSync
distractorClues.sbe: Wrong if no compute needed at edge -> use Snowball. Wrong for continuous -> use DataSync
distractorClues.snowmobile: Wrong for small datasets -> use Snowball. Wrong for continuous -> use DataSync
distractorClues.datasync: Wrong if no internet/Direct Connect -> use Snowball. Wrong for SFTP -> use Transfer Family
distractorClues.transfer: Wrong for large offline transfer -> use Snowball. Wrong for continuous file sync -> use DataSync

database-migration.title: AWS DMS vs AWS SCT vs AWS Backup vs S3 Cross-Region Replication
database-migration.subtitle: Compare database migration and data replication services

values: Replicate S3 objects to another region
All supported AWS resources (databases, EBS, S3, etc.)
"Migrate MySQL to Aurora with minimal downtime"

studyAids.mnemonic: DSCB: D(MS=database migration) + S(CT=schema conversion) + C(entralized=AWS Backup) + B(ackup+S3 CRR). Databases Safely Converted, Backed.
studyAids.studyTips: DMS = migrate data with minimal downtime (continuous replication). SCT = convert schema between engines. AWS Backup = centralized backup management. S3 CRR = cross-region disaster recovery.

examKeywords.dms: Database migration, Minimal downtime, Continuous replication, CDC
examKeywords.sct: Schema conversion, Heterogeneous, Oracle to PostgreSQL, Code conversion
examKeywords.backup: Centralized, Policies, Cross-region backup, Retention, Compliance
examKeywords.crr: S3, Cross-region, Async, Disaster recovery, Replication

distractorClues.dms: Wrong for schema conversion -> use SCT. Wrong for file sync -> use DataSync
distractorClues.sct: Wrong for data migration -> use DMS. Only for schema
distractorClues.backup: Wrong for database migration -> use DMS. Wrong for S3 replication -> use CRR
distractorClues.crr: Wrong for database migration -> use DMS. Wrong for file sync -> use DataSync

global-infrastructure.title: Regions vs Availability Zones vs Edge Locations vs Local Zones vs Outposts
global-infrastructure.subtitle: Compare AWS global infrastructure components

values: Geographic area with multiple data centers
Isolated data centers within a Region
Bring AWS closer to users in specific metros

studyAids.mnemonic: RAELO: R(egions=geographic) + A(Zs=data centers) + E(dge=CDN) + L(ocal Zones=metro) + O(utposts=on-prem). Regions Are Everywhere, Locally Optimized.
studyAids.studyTips: Regions = geographic areas. AZs = isolated data centers within a region. Edge locations = CDN/CloudFront. Local Zones = metro proximity. Outposts = on-premises AWS.

examKeywords.region: Geographic, Data sovereignty, Latency, Multiple AZs
examKeywords.az: Isolated, High availability, Fault tolerance, Data center
examKeywords.edge: CloudFront, CDN, Low latency, Caching, 400+ globally
examKeywords.lz: Metro, Proximity, Single city, Ultra-low latency
examKeywords.outpost: On-premises, Hybrid, Low latency, Full AWS services

distractorClues.region: Wrong when asking about data center isolation -> use AZ
distractorClues.az: Wrong for global content delivery -> use Edge locations
distractorClues.edge: Wrong for compute -> use Region or AZ
distractorClues.lz: Wrong for global content delivery -> use Edge locations. Wrong for general compute -> use Region
distractorClues.outpost: Wrong if just need compute in AWS -> use Region/AZ

cloud-models.title: On-Premises vs IaaS (Amazon EC2) vs PaaS (Elastic Beanstalk) vs SaaS (e.g., Office 365)
cloud-models.subtitle: Compare cloud computing service models by what you manage

values: "Manage OS and above, AWS manages the rest"

studyAids.mnemonic: OIPS: O(n-Premises=manage everything) + I(aaS=manage OS+) + P(aaS=manage app+) + S(aaS=provider manages all). On-Prem Is Painful, SaaS Saves.
studyAids.studyTips: On-Premises = manage everything. IaaS = manage OS and above. PaaS = manage application and data. SaaS = provider manages everything. More PaaS/SaaS = less control but less overhead.

examKeywords.onprem: Full control, Highest cost, Highest overhead, Data center, Servers
examKeywords.iaas: EC2, Manage OS, Flexible, High control, AWS manages hardware
examKeywords.paas: Elastic Beanstalk, Manage app, No server management, Deploy code
examKeywords.saas: Office 365, Fully managed, No infrastructure, Subscription

distractorClues.onprem: Wrong if want to reduce management overhead -> use IaaS/PaaS/SaaS
distractorClues.iaas: Wrong if do not want to manage OS -> use PaaS
distractorClues.paas: Wrong if need OS-level control -> use IaaS
distractorClues.saas: Wrong if need customization/control -> use PaaS or IaaS
